<!-- Topic 3: Dynamic Memory -->
<!-- Slides 51–68 -->

# Dynamic Memory
<!-- Slide 51 -->

## When the Data Changes the Size {.smaller}

- Heap pseudodynamic memory chooses a size when the allocation is created.
- Truly dynamic memory changes allocation size as the program's data changes.

The program does not ask the user for a new length each time. The data operation itself creates the need for a different array size.

::: notes
Slides 51–68
This topic is organized around `rdaa.cpp`, the real dynamically allocated array example.
:::

<!-- Slide 52 -->

---

## rdaa.cpp {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
{{< include assets/rdaa.cpp >}}
```

This file is the complete example that the next slides dissect.

<!-- Slide 53 -->

---

## The rdaa.cpp Story

1. Build an array with one value.
2. Add a second value by creating a larger array.
3. Remove a value by creating a smaller array.

Each size change is really a new allocation plus a transfer of the values that still matter.

<!-- Slide 54 -->

---

## Start With One Element

```{.cpp}
int* pInt = nullptr;
int size = 1;

pInt = new int[size];
pInt[0] = 10;
```

At this moment, `pInt` owns a heap array with exactly one element.

<!-- Slide 55 -->

---

## Grow in Response to New Data

```{.cpp}
int* pTemp = nullptr;
size++;
pTemp = new int[size];

pTemp[0] = pInt[0];
pTemp[1] = 20;
```

The new value `20` requires a larger array. The old value is copied before ownership changes.

<!-- Slide 56 -->

---

## Transfer Ownership After Copy

```{.cpp}
delete[] pInt;
pInt = pTemp;
pTemp = nullptr;
```

`pInt` becomes the owner of the larger array only after the old array has been released.

<!-- Slide 57 -->

---

## Shrink When Data Is Removed

```{.cpp}
size--;
pTemp = new int[size];
pTemp[0] = pInt[0];

delete[] pInt;
pInt = pTemp;
pTemp = nullptr;
```

Removing data from the right means the retained values fit in a smaller allocation.

<!-- Slide 58 -->

---

## Finish the Ownership Contract

```{.cpp}
delete[] pInt;
pInt = nullptr;
```

After the final print, `pInt` still owns a heap allocation. The program should release that last array before it ends.

<!-- Slide 59 -->

---

## Trace the Size Changes

| Moment | Owner | Size | Stored values |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Start | `pInt` | 1 | `10` |
| After grow | `pInt` | 2 | `10 20` |
| After shrink | `pInt` | 1 | `10` |

**Predict:** what would print after each stage?

<!-- Slide 60 -->

---

## Why Resizing Needs a New Array

- An existing allocation cannot grow or shrink in place through C++ syntax.
- Resizing means allocating a different block and preserving the desired values.
- Ownership transfers only after the copy succeeds.

<!-- Slide 61 -->

---

## The Resize Algorithm

1. Decide the new length from the data operation.
2. Allocate a temporary array.
3. Copy the elements that still belong.
4. Release the old array.
5. Point the owner to the new array.
6. Update the tracked length.

<!-- Slide 62 -->

---

## How Many Elements Should Be Copied?

```{.cpp}
int copyCount = oldLength;
if (newLength < oldLength)
    copyCount = newLength;
```

The general rule is `min(oldLength, newLength)`.

<!-- Slide 63 -->

---

## Add One Element {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
void append(int*& values, int& length, int value)
{
    int* temporary = new int[length + 1];

    for (int i = 0; i < length; i++)
        temporary[i] = values[i];

    temporary[length] = value;
    delete[] values;
    values = temporary;
    temporary = nullptr;
    length++;
}
```

Both pointer and length use reference parameters because the function changes the caller's state.

<!-- Slide 64 -->

---

## Remove the Last Element

```{.cpp}
bool removeLast(int*& values, int& length)
{
    if (length == 0)
        return false;

    int* temporary = nullptr;
    if (length > 1)
        temporary = new int[length - 1];

    for (int i = 0; i < length - 1; i++)
        temporary[i] = values[i];

    delete[] values;
    values = temporary;
    length--;
    return true;
}
```

<!-- Slide 65 -->

---

## Resize to Any Length {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
void resize(int*& values, int& length, int newLength)
{
    if (newLength < 0 || newLength == length)
        return;

    int* temporary = nullptr;
    if (newLength > 0)
        temporary = new int[newLength]{};

    int copyCount = length;
    if (newLength < length)
        copyCount = newLength;

    for (int i = 0; i < copyCount; i++)
        temporary[i] = values[i];

    delete[] values;
    values = temporary;
    length = newLength;
}
```

<!-- Slide 66 -->

---

## The Cost of Resizing

- Allocating a new block requires work proportional to the requested storage.
- Copying retained elements takes O(N) time.
- Growing by one element repeatedly can perform many unnecessary copies.

<!-- Slide 67 -->

---

## Summary

- Dynamic memory changes the allocation when the program's data requires a different size.
- A resize is allocate, copy, release, transfer ownership, and update length.

<!-- Slide 68 -->
